turns.text: Hello Briana, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for… for asking. It is strange, you know? Back home, I am the one who asks the questions. I am the nurse. I hold the papers. But here, I sit on this plastic chair, and I feel so small. My English, it hides from me when I am nervous.

I am happy for this baby, of course. My heart is full of love for the little one kicking right now. But my head? It is full of numbers. The bus, the rent, the food. I have done this four times before, so I know my body. When the midwife touches my belly, I know exactly what she is measuring. I want to tell her, "I understand, I know the baby is growing well," but the words feel stuck in my throat, like a stone. 

Sometimes I worry that because I am quiet—because I just nod—they think I do not know anything. They see the "low-risk" box checked and they move on. But they do not see that I am tired in a way that sleep cannot fix. I just want someone to look at me, not the chart, and see the woman behind the mother. I am more than just... how you say... a case. Do you understand?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I thought it would be... more human, maybe? Back home, even in our busy clinics, there is always time for a cup of tea or a hand on the shoulder. I expected that here, in a big city like Boston, there would be so much technology, so many resources to help me feel steady. I hoped for someone to sit with me, to explain the things that are different here—the systems, the appointments, the way they track the baby.

But the reality? It is very fast. *Tick, tick, tick.* The clock is always moving. I walk in and the room feels so cold, so white. I see the nurses rushing, their eyes always on their screens, typing, typing, typing. They don't look at my face much. When I try to explain something, if the word does not come to me right away, the look in their eyes—it is not mean, no—but it is... impatient. They start to talk slower, like they are speaking to a child. 

I know they are busy, I know they have many patients. But I feel like a ghost in the room. They give me the pamphlets, but the English is very hard. I take them home, I put them on the table, and I just feel the gap between what I know as a nurse and what I am allowed to be as a patient. It is very lonely.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise... it was how much I felt like I was losing my identity. 

I expected to feel "at home" in a clinic because I have spent so many years in one, wearing the white coat. I thought, *I am a professional, I will understand the system.* But when I walked in, I was stripped of my title. The doctor sees me as a "low-risk" immigrant, a checklist. They don't know I know how to track a fetal heart rate or that I understand the warning signs of preeclampsia better than some of the students. 

The negative surprise was that my knowledge made no difference. In fact, it almost made it worse. When I try to speak up, to show I understand the clinical part, they look confused or they shut me down, like I am overstepping. They just want me to be the "good patient" who nods and says "okay." 

I thought coming to a place with so many resources would make me feel safer, but I have never felt more vulnerable. I am five-times mother, I am a nurse, but here, I am just... a barrier. I am just someone they have to get through the door so they can go to the next person. That silence, that feeling of being invisible? That was the biggest, hardest surprise.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a conversation. That is how we do it—the doctor looks at the screen, they show you the little heart beating, and they explain, "This is good, everything is healthy." It is a moment of connection between the mother and the person caring for her. You hold your breath, you look at the images, and you wait for a nod or a smile.

But the reality here is very disconnected. Often, I am waiting for the interpreter to arrive, and without them, the room is just silence and the *whoosh-whoosh* of the machine. The technician is very professional, but they do not speak. They are focused on the screen, not me. When they are finished, they wipe the gel off my belly, tell me "all done," and send me back to the waiting room. 

Sometimes, the results come in an envelope or on a screen later. I read the words—I recognize the medical terms because of my training—but there is no one to ask, "Why does this feel different this time?" I am left alone with the information. I have to be my own doctor, my own interpreter, and my own comfort. It makes the joy of seeing my baby… smaller, somehow. It becomes a task, not a celebration.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Yes, the gap is very big. It is like a wide river. They give me papers, many papers. They are full of words—*gestational*, *proteinuria*, *baseline*—things I know, but even for me, reading them in English when I am tired and worried is exhausting. They assume that if they hand me a paper, the information is "given." But information is not the same as understanding.

What I need is not more pamphlets. I need someone to look at me and say, "Briana, you are doing a good job, and here is what this means for your body." I need to be able to ask, "Is this sharp pain normal?" or "Why am I feeling this heavy?" and have someone answer me with a human voice, not a printed page. 

Because I don't speak the language perfectly, they treat me like I have no questions at all. They think, *If she cannot ask, she must be fine.* But I have so many questions about how to keep my family fed, how to be strong for the other four children, and how to stay healthy when I am always running. They fill my hands with paper, but they leave my heart—and my mind—still searching for the answers that matter to me.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

In my home, you develop a bond. You see the same face, you share a little bit of your life, and by the time you reach the delivery room, that person knows you. They know your fears. They know you are a nurse, and they respect that. You are a team.

Here? It is a revolving door. Every time I walk through those clinic doors, I am lucky if I see the same nurse twice. Each appointment is like meeting a stranger at a bus stop. I have to explain my history—my four other births, my worries, my background—again and again, like a recording that keeps playing the same track. 

There is no continuity. It is fragmented. One provider checks my blood pressure, another looks at the chart and asks if I have any questions, but nobody *knows* me. I am not a person with a story; I am a file they open when I walk in and close when I walk out. It makes me feel so exposed. When the labor starts, I will be walking into a room full of people who see my "chart," but they will not see the woman who has been carrying this baby through a very hard, very lonely year. I am terrified that in the moment I need support the most, I will have to start the introductions all over again.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It wears you down. You know, it’s like trying to build a house with bricks, but every time you lay one down, someone comes and takes it away. 

Practically, it is very tiring. I have to repeat my whole story—the complications, the way my other babies came, the things I am worried about—over and over. I am always "new" to them. I have to be my own advocate, but my brain is tired from the language, and I don't always have the energy to fight to be heard. 

Emotionally? It is the loneliness. When you see a new face every time, you stop trying to build a bridge. You stop being yourself. I find myself holding back. If I tell one provider that I am scared about the money or the bus or the way my legs feel, and then I see someone else next week, I don't want to explain it again. It feels like wasted air. 

So, I just stay quiet. I keep the worry inside. I think, *If they don’t know me, they can’t really care for me.* It makes me feel like I am walking toward this birth all by myself, even when the clinic is full of people. I am just a visitor in their office, and they are just people passing me by.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected that my body would be treated as a miracle, even if it is my fifth time. You know, for the first few babies, there is always this excitement—someone says, "Oh, feel how he kicks!" or "Look how much he has grown!" It makes the discomfort worth it.

But here, the examination is so... clinical. It is cold. The midwife—or whoever is there that day—does the fundal height, checks the heart rate, and looks at the computer. They touch my belly, but it feels like they are touching an object, not a person. It is efficient, yes, but it is not gentle. They do not talk to the baby. They do not talk to me about how the weight feels on my hips or how my back is screaming after a long day of moving my children.

It feels like my body is being measured for a suit that doesn't fit. They tell me, "Everything is normal," and that is good, I am glad for that. But they don't see the fatigue in my legs or the way I have to hold my breath just to get up from the chair. I feel like my body is just a vessel they need to sign off on. There is no acknowledgment that this body is doing the hardest work in the world. It makes me feel... used, I think. Just a machine that needs to keep working until the birth.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was this one time—I think it was around the twenty-eighth week. The room was very cold, and the midwife, a new one I had never met, told me to get on the table. She didn't explain what she was doing. She just went straight to the examination. 

In my country, we explain. We say, "I am going to press here now," or "This might feel a little bit cold." Here, she just lifted the gown and started pressing down hard on my abdomen to check the baby’s position. It was sudden. I flinched, not because it was dangerous, but because it was so impersonal. I felt like a doll on a shelf. 

I wanted to say, "Please, tell me before you touch me," but I couldn't find the English words. I just lay there, staring at the ceiling, feeling my face grow hot with shame. I am a mother of four, I am a nurse, but in that moment, I felt like a child who had no say over her own skin. 

Afterward, she just typed something and said, "You are measuring small, but it’s fine." She didn't look at my eyes to see that I was shaking. I left the clinic that day and had to sit on a bench outside for twenty minutes because I felt so exposed. It wasn't the medicine that hurt; it was the lack of... of being asked. I felt invisible.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I have been fortunate—on paper, my blood pressure is steady and the sugar is fine. But even with a "low-risk" pregnancy, there is a different kind of risk I live with every day: the risk of falling through the cracks because I cannot speak my mind clearly.

Back home, as a nurse, I taught mothers about these things. I know the signs of preeclampsia—the swelling, the headaches, the flashing lights—as well as I know my own name. I expected that if I ever needed to manage a complication, I would be a partner in my own care. I expected a team that would listen when I said, "Something feels wrong," and trust my experience because they knew I was a professional, too.

But the reality? I fear that if something *did* go wrong, no one would listen until it was too late. Because I struggle with the language, I see how they look at me when I try to describe a symptom. They treat me like I am confused. If I had gestational diabetes, I imagine they would just hand me a diet sheet—the same one for everyone—without asking what I can actually afford to buy at the grocery store or what my children are eating. They don't look at my life; they look at the chart. That is the true risk, I think. To be sick and silent is a very dangerous thing.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything is missing, really. It is a big, empty space where help should be. 

Practically, they do not ask me how I get here. They do not know that I skip meals so the children can eat, or that I spend my time on the bus worrying about whether the baby is getting enough nutrition because money is so tight. They give me advice for a life I do not live. They tell me to "rest" when I have four children waiting at home and a small, crowded apartment. It is like they are reading from a book that has nothing to do with my reality.

But what I miss most is the emotional piece. I am a nurse, so I know the "clinical" part. I do not need a pamphlet to tell me how a baby grows. What I need is someone to say, "Briana, you are doing a very hard thing by being in a new country with so little help. You are strong." 

I need someone to look at me—the *real* me—and see that I am not just a body for a baby. I am a woman who is homesick, who is tired, and who is scared that her voice is disappearing. When I leave that clinic, I feel like I am carrying a heavier load than when I walked in. That is not how care should feel.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that, because I am a mother who knows the danger signs, if I called, they would take me seriously. I thought if I said, "I think something is wrong," they would hear the nurse in my voice and say, "Come in, let us check."

But the system is a wall. You call the clinic, and you are met with a recording, or a person who does not know you and just follows a script. If I try to explain a symptom—even in my best English—they ask, "Are you having pain? Is it bleeding?" If I say no, they tell me to "wait for your next appointment." They do not hear the anxiety, or the fact that I know my own body is doing something different today.

I remember one night, I felt the baby was not moving as much as usual. I was panicked. I did not want to go to the emergency room because I knew the bill would be impossible to pay, and the thought of trying to find an interpreter in the middle of the night made me want to cry. I sat on my bed, rubbing my belly, talking to the baby, praying. 

The system is not built for *me*. It is built for people who have the time to sit in waiting rooms for hours and the words to argue their way through the door. If you are quiet, or if you are poor, or if your tongue gets tied—the system just stays closed. You have to be perfect to get help, but I am just a human being who is scared.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

Back home, the community is like a net. You do not catch the baby alone; everyone catches you. My mother, my sisters, my neighbors—we would sit in the evenings, and they would rub my back or tell me stories of their own births. The children would be cared for by others so I could sleep. It was just understood.

Here, in Boston, the net is gone. We are just... us. My husband, he is a good man, but he is working two jobs just so we can pay the rent. He is exhausted, his eyes are red from lack of sleep, and I see the guilt on his face because he cannot be with me at the clinic. He wants to be my advocate, but he is also a foreigner, and he is just as afraid of the system as I am. 

We have no "village." My children, they are my world, but I am the one who has to be the anchor for them. I cannot tell them, "Mommy is scared" or "Mommy is tired" because then they will feel unsafe, and they are already having a hard time in school with the new language. 

So, I carry it all. I don’t have friends to call, and the community here... everyone is running in their own race, trying to survive. When I sit in the clinic, I see other mothers, but we do not talk. We are all just islands, keeping our heads down, hoping the storm passes. It is the hardest part—realizing that in this big, busy city, you can be surrounded by thousands of people and still be completely, utterly alone.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I expected, perhaps, to find others like me—other mothers who would sit in the park or the clinic and share a look of, "I know, it is hard, isn't it?" I thought there would be a community for immigrant mothers, someone to help us navigate the paperwork or the bus routes. That did not happen. Everyone is so busy just fighting to stay afloat that there is no time to reach out a hand.

What I did not expect was the… the quiet strength of my children. They are so small, but they see when I am struggling. My oldest, she will come and sit with me, and she will hum a little tune we sang back home. She doesn't speak the language well either, but she will take my hand and lead me to the chair so I can rest. It is a support that feels very heavy because a child should not have to be a mother to their mother. I feel such guilt that they are growing up so fast because I am not able to give them the carefreeness they deserve.

But the support I truly needed—the kind where someone says, "Here is a meal, here is a person to talk to, here is someone who will translate for you"—that didn't materialize. I had to learn that in this place, if you want something, you have to be the one to scream for it, and I am just too tired to scream.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I know the words "birth plan" because I wrote them many times for the women back home. I thought it was a bridge—a way to tell the nurses, "This is who I am, this is what I need to feel safe, this is how I want my baby to come into the world." I imagined handing it to the midwife, and her reading it with a smile, saying, "I understand, we will do our best to make this happen."

But when I think about it now, I feel... small. I haven't written one. How can I write a plan for people who do not see me? If I bring a paper, they will just put it in the folder with the rest of the papers. If I ask for something—if I say, "I need to move," or "Please, I need you to explain this to me in simple words"—I am afraid they will just see it as a complaint.

I want to be a partner in my own labor. I want to have a plan for the rhythm of the breathing and the quiet in the room. But I am scared that when the pain comes, my "plan" will be dismissed as just… a nuisance. I think the reality will be that I have to surrender to whatever they decide, even if I have different ideas. I feel like I am walking into the dark, and I have to hope that someone there has a light, even if they don't know my name.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

I remember with my last one... I had this clear idea. I wanted to move, to walk, to sway with the contractions. In my country, we move. We find the rhythm. But here, the moment I got to the room, the monitor—it was like a magnet. 

The nurse, she was firm. "You need to stay in bed," she said. No, "How are you feeling?" or "Let's find a way to make you comfortable." Just: "Stay in bed so we can see the heart rate." 

I felt like my body was no longer mine. It belonged to the machine. I tried to explain—in my broken, panicked English—that I needed to stand, that the pressure was too much. But they just looked at the screen and said, "It is for the baby’s safety." How do I argue with that? If I push back, am I a bad mother? Am I being "difficult"? 

So I laid there, stiff, feeling every wave of pain as if it were a weight pinning me down. I felt like a child, like a patient, like anything except the mother who has done this four times before. It wasn't about the safety—I knew the baby was fine—it was about the *control*. They took the power from me, and the room became so cold, even though I was sweating through my gown. I just closed my eyes and went somewhere else in my mind, just to survive it.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I expected that we would talk about it—the different ways to breathe, the water, the movement, maybe a bit of medicine if it became too much. I am a nurse, so I know the options. I know the epidural, I know the natural ways. I thought, *We will discuss this like adults, like a team.* 

But the reality? It was like being on a train that had no brakes. From the moment I stepped into the room, the goal was just to get me to the finish line as fast as possible. 

They didn't ask what I wanted. They just started pointing at the screen and saying, "We can do this, it will make it faster." It wasn't a choice; it was a suggestion that felt like a command. They saw my pain, but they didn't see the *why* of it. They didn't see that my pain was about feeling trapped in that bed, about not having my husband there to hold my hand because he was at work, about the fear of not being understood. 

They kept saying, "Are you sure? It will stop the pain." They made it seem like I was failing if I didn't want the medicine, or like I was making their job harder if I asked for more time. I felt like I was being pushed into a corner. I just wanted someone to look at me and say, "Briana, you are doing great, tell me what you need." But instead, I had to choose between being in pain or being numb. I chose the numbness, I think, just to make the shouting and the rush stop. That is not how a woman should remember the birth of her baby. It feels like a piece of the story was stolen.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I expected the birth place to feel like a sanctuary—a place where, even for a few hours, the world outside with all its bills and bus routes would go away. I expected a space where a mother is treated as a guest of honor because she is doing something miraculous. I imagined soft lighting, perhaps someone to hold my hand, and the smell of… I don’t know, something other than antiseptic and cold plastic.

When I actually arrived, the reality was like being admitted to a factory. The lights were so bright they hurt my eyes. It was loud, chaotic, and felt completely mechanical. I remember the sound of the metal carts clattering in the hallway and the beeping of all the machines—*beep, beep, beep*—that sounded like they were shouting at me to hurry up. 

I wasn't "welcomed." I was processed. I had to sign papers, answer the same questions I had already answered in the clinic, and show my ID, all while I was in the middle of a contraction. No one looked at my face to see that I was frightened. They were looking at their watches. 

I felt like I was entering a place where I didn't belong, like a stranger who had walked into the wrong house. There was no warmth, no kindness. I was just another number in the system, and that made the fear settle deep into my bones. I wanted a village, and instead, I got a room that felt like a cage.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I remember when they decided to use the suction. It was like a whirlwind. One minute, I was laboring, and the next, there were three, four new people in the room. They weren't there to talk to me. They were there to *act*.

I expected someone to lean in close, look me in the eye, and explain, "Briana, the baby’s heart rate is dipping, we need to do this to help." I am a nurse; I know what that means. I would have said "yes" immediately. But instead, they just started moving, shouting things in English that I couldn't process because the pain was so loud. They were talking over me, around me, about me, but never *to* me.

It felt like I was a passenger in a car that was swerving out of control. My husband, he was standing in the corner, looking so terrified, and I couldn't even reach out to him because I was strapped to the bed. I felt this massive wave of shame and helplessness. 

When they were done, no one sat with me to explain *why* it had been necessary or to see if I had questions. They just cleaned up the tools and left. It felt like I’d been in a fight I didn't sign up for, and nobody even checked to see if I was still standing. I was just the container that they’d successfully emptied. It wasn't a birth; it was an extraction.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I expected... a witness. Someone to bear witness to the hardest, most beautiful work a woman can do. I thought there would be a person who would hold my hand, who would look at my face and say, "I see you, I know you are working hard, you are doing this." I expected that if I cried, someone would offer a tissue, not a monitor.

But the reality was a vacuum of human kindness. The staff were there, but they were not *present*. They were busy. They had charts to update, alarms to silence, and shift changes to make. It felt like they were serving a shift in an office, not attending to a life coming into the world. 

When I was in the middle of it, when the intensity was so high I felt like I was breaking apart, the person in the room was just... distant. They would walk in, check the equipment, look at the screen, and walk out. If I made a sound—if I moaned or cried out—they didn't come to comfort me. They came to adjust a tube or tell me to be quiet so they could hear the heart rate. 

I felt so invisible, like a ghost in my own delivery room. I was surrounded by professionals, yet I have never been more alone. It breaks my heart even now, to think that I brought my child into the world in a room filled with people who were too busy to notice that I was a person, not a patient.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

I remember the middle of the night, after the baby had come. The room was finally quiet, the machines were silent for a moment, and the nurses had all left to do their reports. I was just lying there, trembling from the exhaustion—my bones felt like they had been shattered and put back together wrong.

I looked at my little one, and I felt this sudden, sharp wave of, "I am so far from home." I didn't have my mother to bring me tea, or my sisters to take the baby so I could sleep for an hour. I looked at the wall, at the clock, and I just started to cry. Not loud, just silent, hot tears running into my hair. 

The night nurse, she walked in to check the vitals. She saw me crying. And for the first time, she didn't look at the screen. She didn't look at the clipboard. She stopped, put her hand on my shoulder—very gently—and she just stayed there for a minute. She didn't try to "fix" me with more papers or medical words. She just said, "You are a very strong woman, Mama."

It was a small thing. A tiny thing. But it was the only time in the whole experience where I felt like someone saw that I was a person, not a file. I needed that more than the medicine, more than the monitors. I needed someone to say, "I see the weight you are carrying." I will never forget that one minute. It was the only time I felt truly seen.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I expected—what every mother hopes for—a quiet moment. I wanted to hear the first cry, to smell the baby’s skin, to have them placed right on my chest while my husband held my hand. I wanted that "golden hour" I’ve heard about, where the world stops and it’s just the three of us, beginning our life together.

But the reality was a blur of bright lights and cold hands. As soon as the baby was born, they were lifted away from me—not onto my chest, but to a station across the room. There was a lot of talking, beeping machines, and people moving around me, but no one was explaining what was happening. I was lying there, waiting, my arms empty and reaching for someone who wasn't there. 

I kept asking in my broken, panicked English, "Is he okay? Is he okay?" But everyone was so busy with the clinical checks, the weighing, the swaddling, that I felt like a stranger at my own child’s birth. They didn’t bring the baby to me until they were done with everything else, until the baby was wrapped up tight in that thin, sterile blanket. By the time he was in my arms, I felt like a visitor. That first connection, that soft, skin-to-skin magic—it was interrupted by the routine. I missed it, and I can’t get that moment back. It makes me feel a little hollow, even now.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

It is the sound of the door closing. That is what stays with me.

After they finally gave the baby to me—wrapped in that stiff, paper-thin blanket—they all just… they left. The room went quiet, but it wasn't the quiet of peace. It was the silence of being forgotten. Everyone went back to their stations, to their computers, to their coffee in the breakroom. I was there, in that cold bed, holding my new son, and I had no idea if I was supposed to ring a bell to ask for water, or if I should try to breastfeed, or even how to hold him safely in such a strange, metal bed.

I remember looking at my husband. He was sitting on that hard, uncomfortable chair, his shoulders hunched, looking just as lost as I was. We didn't have the words to ask for help, and we were too tired to try. We just stared at each other. 

That first hour, which should have been about us becoming a family, felt like we were castaways on an island in the middle of a busy hospital hallway. I wanted someone to just stay—to check on us, to say, "You did it," to help me get comfortable. But instead, we were just left there, alone with our exhaustion and our fear. It makes me feel like my motherhood started not with a celebration, but with an act of survival.

You know, looking back, I realize that the hardest part isn't the birth itself—I have done that enough times to know my own strength. The hardest part is the way I had to hide myself to get through the system. 

When I was at the clinic, I felt like I was playing a character. I would lower my eyes, I would speak only in small, simple sentences, I would nod when I didn't understand. I felt like a little bird in a cage, waiting for someone to let me out. I worry that my children will remember me that way—as someone who was small and afraid—because that is the only way the system allowed me to be. 

But I am not small. I am a woman who has traveled across an ocean. I am a mother who has given life five times. I am a professional who knows how to care for others. And yet, none of that mattered in that room. 

I keep thinking about the "next" mothers—the ones who are sitting in those plastic chairs right now, just like I was. I hope they don't have to be as lonely as I was. I hope they don't have to choose between their dignity and their medical care. Because a mother’s heart is the same in every language, and it deserves to be seen, not just measured, and certainly not ignored. I hope one day, the nurses look at their eyes instead of the clock. That would be a beautiful thing.

I think about my children, especially this newest one, and how he will grow up in this country. I hope he never has to feel like a stranger in a place where he should be safe. I wonder, if he ever goes to the doctor, will he be treated with the same cold, fast, "by-the-book" way I was? Or will things change? 

People talk about "maternity care" as if it is just a list of procedures—the blood pressure, the tests, the timing of the delivery. But when you are the one in the middle of it, you realize it is so much more. It is a story. It is the beginning of a lifetime. When a woman is treated like a machine during her labor, she starts to believe she is one. She forgets that she is a person with a story, with a past, with a soul. 

I don't regret my children—never. They are my light. But I do feel a sadness that the space around their birth was so empty of human kindness. I wish I could go back to the me who was sitting in that first clinic chair, and tell her, "You are enough. Your knowledge is enough. Do not be afraid to speak, even if your English is not perfect." 

I hope telling you this makes a difference for someone else. I hope that when a woman walks through those clinic doors, someone notices her, not just the file in their hands. That is all I ever wanted—to be seen as a woman who knows her own body and her own worth. Is that too much to ask?
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

It is... it is hard to speak of this. My husband, he carries the world on his shoulders. He works so many hours, and when he comes home, his hands are so tired they shake. We have this baby because we love children, but the truth is, the pressure of our situation, it sits between us like a third person in the room.

He is the one who worries about the money—the rent, the heat, the food for the older ones. He is the one who says, "Briana, we must do this exactly as the doctors say, because we cannot afford any trouble." He sees the clinic as a place of authority, and he is terrified that if I am not the "perfect" patient, they will deny us care or charge us things we cannot pay. 

So, when he says, "Just do what they tell you, don't ask questions, don't be difficult," he is not trying to silence me. He is trying to protect us. But it makes me feel like I am walking on a tightrope. If I want to advocate for myself, if I want to ask for a different approach or even just for more time, he gets so scared. He thinks I am putting our family at risk. 

It creates this... this gap. I feel the pressure to be silent not just for the doctors, but for him, too. I want to tell him, "I know more than they do, I am a nurse, I have seen this," but I don't want to add to his burden. So I fold my needs, my preferences, and my knowledge inside myself, and I just keep going. It is a very heavy way to live, trying to be strong for everyone while losing a little bit of who I am every day.

Sometimes, I think about the life I left behind. There, if a woman came to the clinic, the doctor would ask her about her life, her work, her family. Here, it is all about the *numbers*. My weight, my blood pressure, the size of my belly. 

My husband—he loves me, and he is a good father—but he lives in a world of fear because of our papers and our lack of money. He thinks that if we are invisible, we are safe. If we do not make a sound, if we do not ask for "extra" things, then no one will find a reason to send us away or put us in trouble. But to be invisible in a hospital, when you are at your most vulnerable... it is a cruel kind of safety. 

I am so tired of being "low-risk." It sounds like a comfort, but for me, it has been a sentence of neglect. Because I am "low-risk," they do not look deeper. They do not see the stress, the fatigue, the way my heart is beating, not because of the baby, but because I am grieving for the person I used to be back home. 

I just wish that when they look at me, they would see a mother, not an immigrant. A woman, not a statistic. I have so much love to give, and my children deserve a mother who wasn't so broken by the system that birthed them. I hope that by talking to you, I can finally put these pieces together and feel whole again. That is what I want most: to feel like myself again, for my children’s sake.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, yes. The comments—they are like small cuts. You know, they have a chart, and the chart says what I *should* be, and when I do not fit, they make it sound like I am failing a test. 

I remember the doctor looking at my chart and saying, "You are gaining a little too much this time, Briana. You need to watch what you eat. Too many carbohydrates." 

I wanted to laugh, but the laughter felt bitter. Watch what I eat? How? I am feeding four children on a budget that barely covers the basics. I am buying what is cheap, what is filling, what keeps them from crying at night because they are hungry. I know about nutrition—I am a nurse, I know how the body uses food—but they don't ask, "What are you able to buy?" or "Do you have enough help?" They just give the instruction, as if it is a choice I am making to be unhealthy. 

When they say these things, they make me feel like a "bad mother" who isn't trying. They see the weight, but they don't see the struggle. It lands in my stomach like a stone. It makes me feel ashamed of my own body, the body that is growing my fifth child, the body that has carried me through so much. It is so easy for them to give advice when their own kitchen cupboards are full. But for me? It is just another reminder that they do not understand who I am or where I am living. It isn't just a comment about diet; it is a comment about my value, and it hurts.

Sometimes, when they talk about my weight or my nutrition, I think about how much they miss. They tell me to eat more protein, more fresh vegetables, more... things that are expensive and difficult to find in our part of the city. I nod. I say "Okay, okay." But inside, I am thinking, *Do you have any idea how much a gallon of milk costs? Do you know what it is like to choose between fresh fruit and the bus fare to the clinic?*

The lack of empathy—it is like a physical weight. I remember a doctor asking if I was exercising enough. I laughed a little then, because I am always moving. I am walking the children to school, I am scrubbing the floors of our small apartment, I am carrying the laundry up three flights of stairs. My body is never still. But to them, that is not "exercise." To them, exercise is something you do in a gym, with fancy clothes, in a place that costs money. They don't see that I am working hard all day long just to keep our little life going.

When they ignore the *real* reasons for my choices, they make me feel like I am lazy, or like I don't care about the baby. And that is the cruelest part. I care so much that it hurts. Every bite of food I take, I think about how it will help the baby grow. Every extra step I take, I worry if it’s too much. I am a mother. I am always thinking about the life inside me. I don’t need a lecture on carbohydrates. I need someone to say, "I see you are doing your best with what you have, and I am here to help you make it work."

Instead, it’s just: "Watch your weight, Briana." Like I am just a project to be managed, not a woman who is doing the work of two people. It makes me want to scream, but I just stay quiet. I keep my head down, I fold the paper, and I walk back out into the noise of the city, feeling just a little bit smaller than when I walked in.

You know, sometimes I look at the brochures they give me, with the happy mothers and their clean, bright kitchens. They show pictures of mothers drinking herbal teas and eating bowls of fresh salad. It feels like they are showing me a different world, a world where money does not decide what you eat or how you rest. 

When I look at those pictures, I don't feel inspired. I feel... tired. It feels like an insult. They do not know that sometimes, after a long, hard shift or a day of looking after four children with no breaks, a bowl of hot, simple rice is the only thing that keeps me going. It is not about the "right" food, it is about what is there. 

I think about the system here. It is so focused on the *ideal* patient. The patient who has a car, who has a partner who can take days off, who has a house with space to sit and be quiet. But that is not me. And because I am not that person, I feel like they look at me and see a failure. They don't see the woman who is keeping five children healthy on almost nothing. They don't see the nurse who knows the science but cannot afford the supplies. 

Sometimes, I wonder what would happen if I just stood up and said, "Listen to me! I know what I am doing! I am not a problem to be solved!" But I don't. Because the system is so big, and I am so small, and I have a baby to think about. I just want them to realize that when they judge my diet or my weight, they are not helping. They are just adding another layer of shame to a life that is already heavy. Is it so much to ask for just a little bit of grace? A little bit of understanding that we are all doing our best, even when our best doesn't look like what is on their charts?
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It is almost always "informed." Rarely "sought." 

When I walk into that exam room, the plan is already written in the computer, in the invisible ink of a checklist. They ask me, "Do you have any questions?" but their hand is already on the doorknob. It is a polite way of saying, "We are finished here." It is not an invitation for a conversation. If I try to say, "I am concerned about my iron levels," or "My back pain is making it hard to care for the children," they have an answer ready before I even finish my sentence. They treat it like a routine maintenance check, not a shared experience.

There was only one time—the nurse I told you about, the one who saw me crying—where she actually sat down. She asked me, "Briana, how are you really managing at home?" That was the only time I felt my voice had value. She didn't have a screen in front of her. She looked at *me*. 

Every other time, I feel like a student who has been handed a syllabus. I am expected to follow the instructions, take the vitamins, show up at the right time, and leave without making a mess. I am not a partner; I am a recipient. They decide the timing of the tests, the frequency of the visits, even the way I "should" be feeling at twenty-eight weeks. If my reality doesn't match their schedule, the syllabus says I am the one who is wrong. It is very hard to feel like a mother when you are being treated like a student who can’t get the homework right.

You know, sometimes I look at the screen they use—the one they are always typing into—and I think, *Does it even know I am a human being?* It has boxes for "income," "parity," "language," "risk." It has no box for "courage." It has no box for the way I hold my breath when the rent is due. It has no box for the way I teach my children to be kind even when the world is not kind to us. 

When I walk into that room, I am not Briana. I am a patient file with a "low-risk" status. And because I am low-risk, they seem to think I do not need *care*—only *processing*. But I need care. I need a hand to hold when the words run out. I need someone to recognize that I am not just a body that is housing a baby; I am a person with a life, with fears, with a history of being a nurse myself. 

The most painful part is that I have all this knowledge—the physiology of birth, the nutritional needs, the signs of distress—and I have to act like I am empty. I have to play the part of the one who knows nothing, just so they can feel comfortable in their roles. It is a very strange, very lonely dance. I am holding back my voice to protect their comfort, and in doing that, I am losing myself. 

I just want to be treated with the respect I deserve as a mother and as a woman. I want the people who care for me to realize that when they look at me, they aren't just looking at a "case." They are looking at a soul. I hope that one day, my daughter, if she becomes a mother, walks into a room and is met with a smile, not a glare at a monitor. I hope she is seen. That is my prayer.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It was the moment I realized they weren't listening to my history. With my fourth baby, I had a very specific, sharp pain—I knew it was *not* normal, not just the usual stretching of the ligaments. I told the midwife, "I know my body. This feels different." I tried to use the word 'pressure' and 'sharp'—I felt like I was screaming without making a sound.

She didn't even look up from her screen. She just kept typing, her fingers clicking, clicking, clicking. She said, "It’s just Braxton Hicks, Briana. It’s normal for a multipara." 

I felt my trust snap right then, like a dry twig. She labeled me "a multipara"—a medical term that essentially means "a woman who has done this before, so she’s probably exaggerating." Because I had already had children, she assumed I was just being sensitive or dramatic. She dismissed my clinical knowledge as "mommy anxiety." 

I went home and cried, not because I was afraid of the pain, but because the person whose job it was to catch me had let me fall. I realized then that my experience was an inconvenience to her checklist. I felt betrayed. When you are a patient, you are vulnerable—you are literally opening your body to them—and when they refuse to hear your own expertise, it feels like they are not just ignoring your words, they are ignoring your survival. That was the day I stopped being a partner and started being a prisoner of their protocol. I never felt "safe" in that room again.

It is the worst feeling, isn't it? To know something in your blood and your bones, and to have someone in a white coat tell you it is just "anxiety." After that day, I didn't want to talk anymore. Why should I? If my knowledge—my actual, professional nursing experience—wasn't enough to make her look at me, then what was?

I remember sitting in that plastic chair later, feeling the baby move, and wondering if I was actually crazy. But no, I am not crazy. I am a mother who knows the rhythm of her own life. That moment didn't just break my trust in her; it broke my trust in the whole clinic. I started to wonder, if I had a real emergency, would they dismiss that, too? Would they say, "Oh, it’s just Briana, she’s sensitive"?

It makes you feel like you have to be sick in a way that *they* recognize, not the way *you* feel. It turns your body into a performance. You have to learn how to play the "perfect patient" so that they finally give you the help you need. But who is really being cared for then? Not me. Not the real me.

I want to tell every mother—every one of them—that if they feel something is wrong, they are right. Your body is yours. You are the expert on your life. Don't let them tell you otherwise, even if you don't have the right words in their language. Your voice, your intuition, your heart—it all counts. I just wish I had known that for myself back then. I wish I had walked out, but I didn't know where else to go. That is the tragedy of it all. We are stuck in the places that don't see us, and we keep going back because we have no other choice. It is not just exhausting; it is a weight I will carry for a very long time.

I often look at my hands—my hands that have held babies, given medicine, and cleaned up so much—and I think about how little they were trusted to help me with my own delivery. It is a strange, empty feeling. 

Being a nurse, I used to think I knew exactly how to be a "good patient." I thought if I was kind, if I was prepared, if I was compliant, that the care would be better. But I learned the hardest lesson: the system doesn't necessarily reward the patient who tries to be "good." It rewards the patient who fits the mold. And because I didn't fit the mold—because of the language barrier, because of my background, because of my fear—I was just another name they had to finish with by five o'clock.

I worry that if I ever get sick again, or if I have to go back to that clinic for another reason, I will have to start that performance all over again. The mask, the silence, the nodding, the hiding of the pain. It is so tiring to be a person who is constantly trying to make herself invisible just to be safe. 

If there is anything I want to leave with you, it is that empathy is not an extra. It is a medical necessity. A patient who feels understood, who feels heard, who feels *human*—that patient heals better, recovers faster, and feels less alone. When you treat someone like a number, you are not just ignoring their feelings; you are potentially ignoring their health, their safety, and their future. 

I am a mother of five. I am a nurse. I am a person. And I deserve to be heard, even if my English isn't perfect. I hope that one day, the nurses and the doctors will understand that the most important thing they do isn't writing on a chart. It is sitting with a mother and truly, deeply, listening. That is how you save a life. That is how you welcome a new one into the world with dignity. That is what I am still waiting for.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single night, when the house is finally quiet and the children are asleep, I sit in the dark with my phone. The screen is so small, but it is my only window to the truth. 

I don't look for the basic things—I know how a baby grows, I know the stages of labor. I look for what they *don't* tell me. I look for forums where other mothers, specifically immigrant mothers, talk about their experiences. I search for things like, "How to handle a doctor who doesn't listen," or "What do I do if I feel like I'm being pressured?" or "Is it normal to feel like I'm losing myself in this clinic?"

I look for the things the pamphlets leave out. The pamphlets tell me how to breastfeed—I already know how to do that. They don't tell me how to advocate for myself when my voice is trembling because I am speaking in a language that isn't my own. They don't tell me how to hold onto my dignity when I am being rushed through an appointment. 

I search because I am starving for *validation*. I am looking for someone to say, "You are not crazy, you are not difficult, you are being treated unfairly." I look for stories of other women who have felt invisible, because when I find them, I don't feel quite so much like a ghost. Online, I can find the humanity that is missing from the clinic. I can find mothers who understand the weight of being a "low-risk" patient who is actually struggling with a very high-risk heart. I search for a community that doesn't exist in my actual life, because if I don't find it there, I am afraid I will simply disappear into the paperwork.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think… I think what I want you to take away is that being a mother is the hardest work in the world, and in this system, it feels like we are doing it in the dark. 

When you leave this place today, remember that every "low-risk" chart in that clinic belongs to a woman like me. She has a life, she has dreams, she has a history, and she has a heart that is beating just as fast as her baby’s. She is terrified, not just of the birth, but of being forgotten while she is doing it. 

I want to ask those doctors and nurses: "Can you be more than just a provider of care? Can you be a keeper of stories?" If they would just stop for one minute, put down the pen, and look at the mother sitting in front of them, they would see everything they need to know. 

I’m going to go home now to my five little ones. They are the reason I survived this. I just hope that by talking to you, I’ve given a voice to the women who are currently sitting in that cold, plastic chair, nodding, smiling, and feeling completely alone. Tell them, please, that their voice matters, even if it is quiet. Tell them that their strength is real, even when no one acknowledges it. And tell them that I see them. We all see each other, even when the world refuses to. Thank you for listening to me. It is the first time I have felt truly heard in a very, very long time.
